Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd usually wait until I felt like it or until something was coming up, then I'd put in a few hours here and there. I have all the resources I need at home, good internet, quiet space, but I just couldn't bring myself to use them consistently. I'd tell myself I'd study after my extracurriculars, but then I'd be tired and just scroll through my phone instead. It wasn't that I didn't care, I just... drifted through most of it.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd cram when deadlines were breathing down my neck. I never really locked into a set schedule even though I know that would've helped me. The flexibility of online courses made it easy to keep pushing things off. I'd think 'I'll do it tomorrow' and then tomorrow became next week.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about a deadline or a message from a classmate asking about something. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I was missing anything major. I wasn't the type to check in daily out of curiosity or anything like that. If there wasn't an immediate reason, I probably wasn't logging in. The peer support group I was part of helped remind me sometimes, which was nice.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more automatic, especially when we had group discussions going on. My peers would tag me or we'd be working on something together, and that made me check in more regularly. But once those group activities died down, so did my routine. It never really stuck as a habit on my own. I needed that external push from others to keep me accountable.
Q5: The lecture recordings were honestly the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other stuff or replay parts I didn't catch the first time. The discussion forums were also helpful when people were actually active because hearing different perspectives helped things click. I liked being able to listen to content rather than just read walls of text.
Q6: The reading materials and supplementary PDFs, I barely touched those. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit in a folder on my laptop. Anything that required me to sit and read for long periods just didn't happen. I also ignored most of the optional quizzes because they felt like extra work without real consequences. If it wasn't going to affect my grade, I probably skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grade or if it was actually interesting. Like, if I knew the exam would cover specific topics, I'd engage with those materials. Also, anything that involved interaction with other students felt more worthwhile because it broke up the monotony. Sitting alone reading something that might not even be tested felt like a waste.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them, like I knew I had to do them eventually even if I procrastinated. Optional activities? I'd look at them and think 'that would be nice' and then never actually do them. There was no urgency, no consequence, so they just fell off my radar. I wish I'd engaged more with them but I couldn't motivate myself.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone when I was out or doing other things. Sometimes I'd study with friends in person and we'd go over material together without any of us being logged into the LMS. I also used YouTube videos to understand concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be discussing course material with my peer support group in person or over WhatsApp. We'd quiz each other, explain concepts, work through problems together. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I also spent time just thinking through problems in my head during commutes or before bed. That kind of mental processing doesn't leave any digital trace.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download a lecture video once and then watch it multiple times offline over the next few days. Same with any PDFs I actually bothered to save. The LMS would show one access, but I might have reviewed that material three or four times on my own. It was just more convenient to have things saved locally rather than streaming every time.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially when I was feeling down. I've been dealing with some sadness lately, nothing I can't handle, but it made everything feel heavier. During those times, even logging in felt like a chore. I'd look at the assignments piling up and just feel overwhelmed even though I knew I could do them. My extracurriculars also competed for my attention and energy.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one for me. After my extracurricular activities, I'd come home drained and the last thing I wanted to do was engage with coursework. Confusion sometimes made me avoid certain modules because I didn't want to deal with not understanding something. When multiple things were due around the same time, I'd focus on whatever was most urgent and let other stuff slide.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing but I'd be on my phone or thinking about something else entirely. Sometimes I'd click through readings just to mark them as complete without really absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions. My body was there but my mind was somewhere else. Those sessions felt like wasted time afterward.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they were important. Part of it was just not feeling like it, part of it was this weird avoidance thing where if something seemed hard or boring, I'd just... not do it. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure anyway. But really, I think I was just scared of starting and finding out I didn't understand something. It was easier to avoid than to fail.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly I'd be logged in for hours trying to catch up. It wasn't healthy, but it worked enough to get me through. The LMS would probably show these huge spikes in my activity right before things were due.
Q17: Feedback did affect me when I actually got it. If I did well on something, it motivated me to keep going for a bit. If the feedback was negative or confusing, I'd sometimes disengage because I felt like what's the point. The peer support helped here because my friends would help me process feedback and figure out what to do differently. Without them, I might have spiraled more.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work around my schedule, bad because I could also procrastinate endlessly. Having everything available at once made it hard to know where to start. I think I would've done better with more rigid weekly requirements that forced me to stay on track rather than letting me drift.
Q19: The discussion forums being optional made me engage less than I probably should have. If they'd been required, I would have participated more. On the positive side, having audio and video content made things easier for me as an auditory learner. The assignment instructions were sometimes unclear which made me avoid starting them. Clearer guidance would have helped me engage earlier.
Q20: I can totally see how that happens. You might be logging in, watching videos, clicking around, but when it comes to actually producing work, there's a block. Maybe anxiety about submitting something imperfect, or just not managing time well enough to finish things. Activity doesn't equal productivity. Sometimes you're just there without really accomplishing anything meaningful.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I didn't have the highest LMS activity but I still pulled off decent grades because I studied in other ways. I'd listen to lectures offline, study with friends, use external resources. My exam scores were okay because I could absorb information through listening even if I wasn't constantly logged in. The LMS doesn't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, how long you spend on things, what you click on. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't tell if you're actually focused or just have a tab open. They definitely can't capture studying with friends, thinking through problems, or using outside resources. It's a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through things. It's asking questions, thinking critically, connecting ideas. For me, engagement also means feeling some kind of connection to the material or the people in the course. When I was engaged, it was usually because of interactions with peers.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support group meant to my learning experience. Those conversations, the encouragement, the shared struggle - that kept me going when I wanted to give up. It also wouldn't show the sadness I've been carrying or how that affected my motivation even when my stress and anxiety were manageable. The human stuff doesn't show up in data.
